behavioral_basis: These three players contributed in most rounds (5/6) — consistent with a conditional-cooperator strategy I would likely follow: generally contribute to support the group but sometimes withhold if cooperation is low or uncertain.

Their single defections occurred in rounds where several others also defected, suggesting responsiveness to peers rather than pure free-riding; I would behave similarly (cooperate by default, reduce contributions when others do).
None of them are extreme unconditional cooperators or persistent free-riders, matching my tendency to balance social preferences and self-interest across repeated rounds.
uncertainty_notes: I only see contributions and one chat message; motives (intentional reciprocity, errors, or experimentation) are unclear.
Group-level dynamics and private payoffs may have influenced individual choices in ways not visible here.
Several players have very similar patterns (e.g., SLOTH, CHICKEN, RABBIT), so other nearby matches are plausible given limited information.

behavioral_basis: RABBIT: Consistently contributed, encouraged cooperation, and used targeted punishment to deter non-contribution — matches my likely tendency to cooperate while enforcing norms.
SLOTH: Steady full contributions and minimal conflict/punishment — matches a cooperative, non-confrontational style I might adopt if I prefer harmony over policing.
GORILLA: Consistent contributions with occasional punitive responses but low chat engagement — matches a pragmatic, mostly cooperative participant who punishes only when necessary.
uncertainty_notes: Some punishments may reflect reaction to perceived display errors (e.g., OWL's disputed contributions), which might make players appear more punitive than they would be with perfect information.
Chat content is brief and may not fully capture players' private reasoning or off-screen behavior; mapping is an inference from limited actions and messages.

I allowed probabilistic weighting because multiple behaviors (cooperative non-punisher vs. cooperative enforcer) are plausibly how I would act depending on my tolerance for enforcement and confidence in the interface.

behavioral_basis: Start with a moderate contribution (around 10) and increase when others appear more cooperative (rising to 15) — I tend to be a conditional cooperator rather than an unconditional full cooperator.
Avoid committing to full 20 every round (so not identical to SLOTH), but occasionally respond strongly to others' moves (temporary drops or spikes) as shown by GORILLA/DUCK.
Willingness to return to higher contributions after a low round (Gorilla's jump to 20 in round 6) fits my tendency to be forgiving/experimentation rather than permanently defecting.
uncertainty_notes: No chat messages or stated intentions are shown, so motives behind each change (experimentation, error, retaliation, or strategy) are ambiguous.
Small number of rounds and only three players observed — patterns could arise from idiosyncratic one-off moves rather than stable strategies.
I may behave differently if I saw payoffs, communication, or believed players were strategic; observed contributions alone leave room for multiple plausible interpretations.

behavioral_basis: SLOTH: started as a full contributor (20) for several rounds then cut contribution sharply after repeated low contributions from another player — this matches a conditional-cooperator who retaliates by withdrawing cooperation when others free-ride, which is how I would plausibly behave.

GORILLA: always contributed the maximum (20) every round, consistent with an unconditional cooperator or ‘saint’ who values group payoff over short-term gain — I could behave this way but less likely than conditional cooperation.

DUCK: contributed below the maximum throughout and even punished a full contributor once, suggesting inconsistent or noisy strategy and possibly spiteful or mistaken punishment; this is the least like how I would act.
uncertainty_notes: Motivations behind punishment and the timing of contribution changes are not observable (e.g., messages, intentions), so inferred strategy labels (conditional cooperator, unconditional cooperator, spiteful/noisy) are tentative.
Small number of rounds and only three observed players limit confidence; different hidden factors (risk preferences, misunderstanding, retaliation) could explain the same actions.
Punishment is rare in the trace (one 1-unit punish by DUCK) so its interpretation (targeting, error, message) is particularly uncertain.

behavioral_basis: Consistently contributed the full 20 each round (strong pro-cooperation stance).
Regularly rewarded other players—especially contributors—indicating willingness to reinforce cooperative behavior.
Used chat to encourage contribution and explain the benefits of cooperation (prosocial leadership).
CHICK similarly contributed regularly and systematically rewarded contributors, matching a conditional-reward strategy I would use.
SLOTH contributed often and called out non-contributors, which aligns somewhat with my tendency to both cooperate and publicly advocate for cooperation.
uncertainty_notes: Observations are limited to revealed contributions, rewards, and chat — internal motives and off-record decision rules are unknown.
Some players (e.g., SNAKE, CROCODILE) show mixed behavior later in the game that could reflect strategic shifts I might or might not adopt.

I may behave slightly differently depending on group dynamics or if I could target punishments/rewards more precisely; the mapping above reflects the most typical steady cooperative strategy I would plausibly follow.

behavioral_basis: SLOTH: Consistently contributes 20, posts pro-cooperation chat messages, and applies small, targeted punishments and occasional small rewards — matches a cooperative, norm-enforcing style I would use.
SNAKE: Frequently advocates contributing, punishes non‑contributors repeatedly (moderate, targeted punishments), and sometimes rewards cooperators — fits a conditional cooperator who enforces norms.
CHICK: Actively rewards many players and encourages others in chat and later contributes regularly — fits a supportive cooperator who uses positive reinforcement rather than harsh sanctions.
uncertainty_notes: I cannot see players' exact motivations (some punishments may be retaliatory or spiteful rather than norm enforcement).
Contribution history and messaging sometimes conflict (players who chat pro‑cooperation sometimes failed to contribute early).
Punishment magnitudes vary widely across players (e.g., OWL’s very large deductions) so matching is based on typical rather than extreme rounds.

behavioral_basis: Consistent contribution to the public good (cooperative strategy).
Gorilla explicitly explains the collective benefit and does simple payoff math — matches my tendency to cooperate and communicate reasoning.
Duck is friendly, supportive in chat and shifts to cooperation, matching a sociable cooperative style.
Dog is a silent but steady cooperator, which is a plausible but less-identifiable match.
uncertainty_notes: Chat behavior and a few rounds of actions provide limited evidence about underlying motivations.
Parrot’s outspoken style and Sloth’s minimal chat could also match some of my behaviors in different social moods, so assignments are probabilistic.

behavioral_basis: PARROT consistently contributed 20 in most rounds and only used small, infrequent punishments — aligns with a cooperative strategy that favors contribution and only occasional, targeted punishment.
CHICKEN displayed mostly cooperative contributions with occasional punishments (small units) and some early defection — matches a generally cooperative player who punishes selectively.
DUCK shows mixed contributions and occasional retaliatory punishments; plausible as a less consistent cooperator but still engages in targeted punishment when provoked.
uncertainty_notes: Players' hidden motives and the unknown total rounds make behavior interpretation noisy — occasional defections may be strategic (end-game or experimentation).
Punishment patterns can reflect retaliation, preemptive enforcement, or noise; distinguishing these motives from observed actions is uncertain.
Chat messages are sparse and sometimes ambiguous, so alignment is based mainly on contribution and punishment patterns rather than explicit commitments.

behavioral_basis: Consistently cooperative (mostly contributed the maximum once norm established).
Used positive rewards to acknowledge and reinforce cooperation (Gorilla rewarded others, including broad distribution in round 16).
Encouraging and team-oriented chat: supportive messages and morale-building (Parrot, Monkey).
Forgiving/tolerant of occasional mistakes while emphasizing group payoff rather than punitive retaliation.
uncertainty_notes: We only observe revealed contributions, chat, and sparse reward actions — underlying motives are ambiguous.
Reward use is visible only in a few rounds; some players with similar contribution patterns did not use rewards, so matching is imperfect.
Player behavior could vary under different stakes or if identities/roles changed, so alignment is tentative.

behavioral_basis: MONKEY consistently advocated contributing the maximum, cheered cooperators, never used punishment — matches a cooperative, pro-social strategy without costly retaliation.
OWL repeatedly urged full contributions and stayed positive in chat, with no punishments recorded — aligns with steady encouragement and cooperation.
GORILLA mainly pushed for maximum contributions and joined positive group momentum (despite one unclear early chat), showing an emphasis on cooperation rather than punishment.
uncertainty_notes: Players' private motives and any off-chat considerations are unknown; observed actions may not capture all reasoning.
Some players (e.g., SNAKE, DOG) mixed prosocial messaging with punitive actions; it's possible I might sometimes punish late-game defectors, which would shift alignment.
Chat snippets are short and occasional typos make intent ambiguous (e.g., 'contribute least' from GORILLA in round 1 may be accidental).

behavioral_basis: CHICKEN: consistently high contributions, frequent rewarding of others, and active agreement/praise—signals cooperative leadership and reciprocity, which matches how I would play.
DOG: mostly high contributions, agreed to the group plan, and occasional rewarding—reflects steady cooperation and moderate enforcement, similar to my likely style.
PARROT: initiated coordination to contribute fully in the final rounds and used chat to promote cooperation, showing proactive coordination even if contributions were variable earlier.
uncertainty_notes: I only observe revealed contributions, rewards, and chat; internal motives and off-record considerations are unknown.
Some players show mixed behavior (e.g., early free-riding then later cooperation), so alignment is based on overall tendency rather than perfect consistency.
Reward allocations are visible but not recipients' identities in summaries, which limits inference about conditional reciprocity strategies.

behavioral_basis: DUCK: Consistently high contributions, uses both punishments and rewards strategically (including large targeted rewards/punishments), and communicates — matches my likely conditional-cooperator strategy: contribute, but sanction clear free-riders and reward cooperation.
PARROT: Steady full contributions and occasional mild punishments of repeat free-riders (DOG). Quiet, low-cost enforcement fits my tendency to cooperate and only lightly sanction.
GORILLA: Consistently full contributions and minimal engagement in punishment/reward. If I were more reserved about sanctioning, Gorilla’s behavior would match my basic cooperative core.
uncertainty_notes: Actions recorded don’t reveal private motives or the exact decision rule (e.g., whether punishments were retaliatory, pre-emptive, or signaling).
Some players (SLOTH) use extreme, costly punishment that I personally would find too costly — this reduces my alignment with them, but it’s possible I might escalate under provocation.

The game context (e.g., how much weight I place on social norms vs. payoff maximization) could shift my behavior toward either milder or harsher sanctioning, so the match probabilities reflect this uncertainty.

behavioral_basis: Consistent contribution: SLOTH contributed the full amount in every round, matching my preference to cooperate regularly.

Targeted rewarding: SLOTH and CHICKEN both reward others fairly often and sometimes invest multiple reward units in specific players (e.g., SLOTH giving 2–3 units), which fits my tendency to selectively reward helpful/important behavior rather than reward everyone indiscriminately.

Pro-social but not indiscriminate: PARROT contributes almost always and frequently rewards others, showing a generally cooperative, reciprocity-focused style that matches my likely approach (cooperate most rounds and reciprocate/encourage cooperation).
uncertainty_notes: Only contributions and anonymous reward actions are observable — motives (e.g., signaling, experimentation) are ambiguous.

Some players (e.g., COW, SNAKE, OWL) give many small rewards to almost everyone or defect often; these patterns could reflect different but plausible strategies I might adopt in other contexts, so assignment is probabilistic.
Behaviors changed across rounds (especially in late rounds), so the match is based on overall tendency rather than exact round-by-round identity.

behavioral_basis: CROCODILE: consistently contributed and used both rewards and occasional punishments across many rounds (e.g., rewarded CHICK in R1, punished DOG/PARROT/MOOSE in R3, rewarded many players in R14) — matches a strategy of steady contribution plus targeted use of rewards/punishments to sustain cooperation.

GORILLA: frequently rewarded apparent cooperators across early and middle rounds (R1,R2,R3,R4,R5), indicating a cooperative, reinforcement-focused strategy even after a single defection (R2). This fits a conditional-cooperator who emphasizes positive reinforcement.

SLOTH: contributed every round and repeatedly punished clear defectors (e.g., punished CHICK in R1 and R7, punished GORILLA in R2), reflecting a consistent contributing player who uses punishment to discourage free-riding.
uncertainty_notes: Only observed actions (contributions and punishment/reward units) are available; motives (e.g., spite, reciprocity, mistakes) are ambiguous.
Some players had occasional one-off defections that could be errors rather than strategic choices (e.g., GORILLA in R2), making behavioral inference noisy.

Anonymity and lack of direct messages limit ability to infer social targeting or alliances; my self-match is based on typical conditional-cooperator behavior (contribute each round, reward cooperators, punish defectors modestly).

behavioral_basis: Consistent full contributions across nearly all rounds (strong cooperative strategy).
Frequent encouraging/chatty messages urging others to contribute and calling out defections—matches my tendency to both cooperate and promote cooperation.
Quick forgiveness/understanding of apparent mistakes (e.g., apologies for misclicks) and steady return to cooperation—aligned with a cooperative but pragmatic approach.

Players’ private payoffs and any off-platform coordination are unknown; similarity is judged only on the revealed actions and chat.

behavioral_basis: Consistently contributed each round (reliable cooperator), encouraged group cooperation in chat — aligns with my tendency to cooperate and promote coordination (PARROT).

Avoided aggressive moralizing about punishment and expressed willingness to keep group norms by occasional targeted punishment — matches my preference for measured enforcement rather than frequent costly retaliation.
CHICKEN matches my social, encouraging style and reluctance to punish; MOOSE matches a stricter enforcer profile I might adopt only occasionally if free-riding persisted.
uncertainty_notes: Inferring internal motives from observed actions/chat is imprecise; multiple players show overlapping cooperative behaviors.
My own behavior could shift depending on how costly I perceive punishment and whether defections seem accidental vs. intentional, changing similarity to PARROT vs. MOOSE.
Some players (e.g., GORILLA, RABBIT) are low-chat cooperators; if I preferred quiet cooperation over chatty encouragement, my match ranking could change.

behavioral_basis: These three players contributed every round (DOG, FROG, WHALE each contributed 20 in all 10 rounds), which indicates a stable, unconditional cooperation strategy.

Given the payoff structure (private incentive to defect but higher group payoff from universal contribution) I would most plausibly choose a reliably cooperative policy to maximize collective return and avoid trying to 'time' others without communication.

The consistency across rounds suggests a simple rule (always contribute), which matches my preference for straightforward, robust strategies when opponents' future behavior and game length are unknown.
uncertainty_notes: I might instead be a conditional cooperator who adjusts after observing many defections; several players show mixed patterns, so my true behavior could align with a high but not perfect cooperator (e.g., OWL or COW).

Because players couldn't see choices before deciding and the number of rounds was unknown, I could have chosen a more selfish strategy if I prioritized guaranteed private payoff — that would match some defect-heavy players.
Without information about my risk preferences or social preferences in this specific experiment, the assignment above reflects the most straightforward match (always cooperate) but is not certain.

behavioral_basis: Consistently contributes (always 20) — I would cooperate rather than free-ride in this public-goods setting.
Regular, targeted punishment of non-contributors: GORILLA punishes many defectors with modest (mostly single-unit) sanctions, matching my tendency to punish reliably but not excessively.

CROCODILE is also a steady cooperator who punishes repeat defectors with somewhat stronger (multi-unit) sanctions — this matches a plausible variant of my strategy (willing to pay more to deter persistent free-riders).
COW is a consistent cooperator who punishes occasionally and conservatively, matching a cautious pro-social punisher profile I might adopt in some rounds.
uncertainty_notes: I only observe summary actions, not motives or deliberation — identical actions could stem from different preferences (inequity aversion, spite, norm enforcement).
Punishment identities are anonymous to targets and total rounds were unknown to players; those framing details might change how I would actually behave.
Some players occasionally use very large or erratic punishments (e.g., WHALE, DOG) — without context it's hard to know whether I would match those extreme patterns, so I favored moderate enforcers.

Used chat to explain errors, persuade others, and foster teamwork — I would likely do the same to promote the group-optimal outcome.
CHICKEN’s steady cooperation with minimal chat matches a quieter cooperative style I might also show in some sessions.
GORILLA’s consistent cooperation but more procedural questioning reflects a cooperative-but-curious stance I sometimes take.

behavioral_basis: I would mostly withdraw (defect) each round but sometimes contribute fully to encourage cooperation or reciprocate — GORILLA shows a similar mixed pattern (several defections with occasional contributions).
I tend to be social/communicative in experiments; GORILLA sent friendly chat messages, which matches my likelihood of greeting/engaging.
I would be unlikely to use costly punishment given no strong signals of cooperation enforcement; no player used punishment, so non-punishing cooperators/defectors align with my likely behavior.

Chat appearances are sparse; absence of chat doesn't mean lack of social intent — players may behave similarly but not type messages.

behavioral_basis: SLOTH: Consistently moderate contributions (often ~10) with little use of costly rewards — matches a conditional cooperator who prefers contributing but rarely pays to reward/punish.
GORILLA: Frequently high contributions and the most active rewarder — matches a pro-social, enforcement-oriented player who both contributes and uses rewards to shape others' behavior.
CHICKEN: Fluctuating between 0/20 contributions and selectively rewarding (often reciprocating GORILLA) — matches a conditional reciprocator but less consistent than the top matches.
uncertainty_notes: Behavioral motives (e.g., spite, experiment strategy, mistakes) are not directly observable from contributions and rewards alone.
Reward targets sometimes include low contributors, so apparent generosity might reflect attempts to build alliances rather than pure rewarding of cooperators.
Later-round shifts (many zeros late in the game) could reflect end-game effects that would alter how closely any player matches my steady strategy.
